semantic_journey: Arabic balsam = the resin of Commiphora (balsam of Mecca), a prized aromatic. Greek balsamon = balsam. The Semitic root b-l-s-m names this aromatic resin, attested in ancient Hebrew as basam (spice/balsam). Greek borrowed the word from Semitic via trade in the luxury resin. Material culture transmission - an aromatic trade commodity and its Semitic name entered Greek.
key_insight: Semitic balsam term (b-l-s-m) entered Greek via ancient spice trade - confirmed material culture cognate.

semantic_journey: Arabic variant spelling of Samosata and Greek Samosata are the same city. Variant of GRC_0000. Proper place-name identity.
key_insight: Variant of GRC_0000 - another Arabic spelling of Samosata, same toponymic identity.

semantic_journey: Arabic Constantinople (city named after Emperor Constantine) and Greek of Constantine. Both refer to the Byzantine capital. Arabic borrowed from Greek (Konstantinoupolis). Proper name - a Latin personal name plus Greek polis, transmitted Greek-to-Arabic.
key_insight: Constantinople - city name (Latin Constantine + Greek polis), borrowed from Greek into Arabic. Proper name identity.

semantic_journey: Arabic Samuel and Greek Samuel are the same Hebrew personal name transmitted into Greek via the Septuagint. Biblical proper names are excluded by the methodology.
key_insight: Biblical personal name Samuel - known Semitic name transmitted via scriptures, excluded.

semantic_journey: Arabic and Greek are proper place names. Gindarus was a city in northern Syria (Cyrrhestica). Arabic form is a rendering of the same Hellenistic toponym. Proper name identity.
key_insight: Same Hellenistic city name (Gindarus in Syria) rendered in Arabic and Greek - toponymic identity.

semantic_journey: Arabic means anger and vexation (being mixed up in a disturbing way). Greek means having become angry (from bile/gall - the humoral theory that bile = anger). Both encode anger/vexation. Arabic through inner agitation concept; Greek through bile metaphor for anger. The semantic domains align directly at anger as inner disturbance. One direct step.
key_insight: Arabic anger/vexation and Greek angered share the anger semantic domain - plausible direct alignment.

semantic_journey: Arabic and Greek are proper place names from different geographic regions - Glaphyrai was a Thessalian town in Greece; Arabic Jalfar is in the UAE. Geographically unrelated proper names with superficial phonetic resemblance.
key_insight: Two unrelated proper place names (Greek Thessaly vs Arabian Gulf) - accidental phonetic resemblance.

semantic_journey: Arabic bardhawn = horse, riding horse, pack horse (strong working animal). Greek bourdon = mule (hybrid pack animal). Semantic journey: general working/pack animal -> specialized to mule. Arabic preserves the broader term; Greek specialized to the mule specifically. Both share the pack animal field. The term is a wanderwort of Iranian/Turkish origin traveling through trade and military routes. Material culture transmission.
key_insight: Arabic pack-horse term relates to Greek mule term - pack animal vocabulary shared across Mediterranean trade routes.
